chunk_summary: Defines role boundaries and escalation protocols for team-delivered tele-nutrition in India, emphasizing RMP accountability, allied health scopes, and workflow safeguards for patient safety.
learning_objectives: Understand the role boundaries for Registered Medical Practitioners (RMPs) in tele-nutrition teams.
Learn the responsibilities of allied health professionals, including dietitians and nursing staff, in tele-nutrition workflows.
Identify escalation triggers for systemic symptoms, severe anemia, SAM danger signs, or eating disorder red flags.
Explore consent, documentation, and workflow safeguards in tele-nutrition delivery.
key_facts: Only qualified and licensed RMPs can diagnose and prescribe in telemedicine settings.
India’s National Commission for Allied and Healthcare Professions mandates defined scopes and registers for allied health roles.
Health workers can assist with history-taking, patient examination, and reinforcing RMP advice but cannot diagnose or prescribe.
Dietitians must escalate cases with systemic symptoms, suspected SAM danger signs, severe anemia, or eating disorder red flags to RMPs.
Two sign-offs are required in tele-nutrition workflows: diet plan finalization and RMP approval.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Telemedicine guidance is explicit that only qualified and licensed registered medical practitioners (RMPs) may practice telemedicine, which must translate into clear scope-of-practice boundaries in team-delivered tele-nutrition. (Source: Telemedicine Practice Guidelines, India)
document_key_topics: Scope of practice for allied health professionals
